records.title: Feature negotiation must verify device acceptance of the selected feature subset

citation.citation_excerpt:
- Driver MUST set FEATURES_OK status bit after writing feature bits
- Driver MUST re-read device status after setting FEATURES_OK to confirm acceptance

records.summary: The core must set FEATURES_OK, re-read status, and refuse probe if the device rejects the selected feature subset.
records.description: When `virtio_dev_probe()` hands off finalized features, `virtio_features_ok()` in `drivers/virtio/virtio.c:203-235` must confirm device acceptance rather than assuming the transport accepted the subset.
records.user_story: As a transport maintainer bringing a device to DRIVER_OK, I expect feature negotiation to fail loudly when the device rejects the selected subset so that later queue setup never runs on an unaccepted contract.
records.implementation_note: The implementation stages feature negotiation in `virtio_dev_probe()` and confirms acceptance in `virtio_features_ok()`.
conditions_of_satisfaction: Set `VIRTIO_CONFIG_S_FEATURES_OK` before re-reading status (`drivers/virtio/virtio.c:227-229`).
Return `-ENODEV` when the re-read lacks `FEATURES_OK` (`drivers/virtio/virtio.c:229-233`).
Reject modern memory-access configurations that omit `VIRTIO_F_VERSION_1` or `VIRTIO_F_ACCESS_PLATFORM` (`drivers/virtio/virtio.c:210-221`).
alternative_paths: Legacy devices may return early when `VIRTIO_F_VERSION_1` is absent (`drivers/virtio/virtio.c:224-225`).

records.title: Device reset must not return control until transport-visible reset completion is observable

citation.citation_excerpt:
- Driver MUST write 0 to device_status to initiate reset
- Driver MUST wait for device_status read to return 0 before reinitializing

records.summary: Transport reset hooks must provide an observable completion boundary before later setup or recovery continues.
records.description: The core reset entry point at `drivers/virtio/virtio.c:253-267` relies on transports like `vm_reset()` and `vp_reset()` to define when reset is actually complete.
records.user_story: As a driver author resetting a device before reprobe or teardown, I expect reset to finish before control returns so that queue setup and status transitions never race an in-progress transport reset.
records.implementation_note: PCI modern polls `device_status` after writing zero; MMIO currently only triggers reset with a status write.
conditions_of_satisfaction: Each transport reset hook writes `0` to the status register (`drivers/virtio/virtio_mmio.c:255-256`, `drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_modern.c:551-552`).
At least one completion read or poll separates reset trigger from post-reset work (`drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_modern.c:553-559`).
Callers treat `config->reset()` as the completion boundary before continuing (`drivers/virtio/virtio.c:253-267`).
alternative_paths: Queue-local reset under `VIRTIO_F_RING_RESET` still depends on transport-visible completion before re-enable (`drivers/virtio/virtio_ring.c:3382-3399`).

records.title: Configuration-change interrupts must be reported as handled whenever they trigger driver-visible work
records.summary: Interrupt handlers must preserve handled status when config or slow-path work runs, even without regular queue completions.
records.description: The contrasting paths are `drivers/virtio/virtio_mmio.c:292-306` and `drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_common.c:72-124`, where MMIO accumulates handled state and PCI INTx does not.
records.user_story: As a kernel integrator debugging interrupt behavior, I expect the IRQ return value to match the work actually serviced so that config-only and slow-path events are not misclassified as spurious interrupts.
records.implementation_note: MMIO sets `ret = IRQ_HANDLED` for config changes and ORs vring results; PCI INTx calls `vp_config_changed()` but returns only `vp_vring_interrupt()`.
conditions_of_satisfaction: Config interrupts that notify the driver mark the handler as handled (`drivers/virtio/virtio_mmio.c:296-299`, `drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_common.c:73-79`).
Slow-path queue callbacks serviced during config handling count as handled work (`drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_common.c:60-79`).
The final IRQ return path accumulates config-handled state rather than discarding it when no regular queue work exists.
alternative_paths: Shared MSI-X, per-vq MSI-X, and MMIO may deliver the same logical work through different vectors, but all must preserve truthful handled accounting.

records.title: Queue-discovery fallbacks must preserve the same queue numbering as the primary transport path
records.summary: Fallback queue discovery must preserve device-advertised queue numbering, especially for admin queues.
records.description: The MSI-X and INTx admin-queue paths in `drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_common.c:391-394`, `:438-441`, `:468-470`, and `:499-502` diverge on whether they use `avq->vq_index` or `queue_idx++`.
records.user_story: As a maintainer relying on degraded routing to behave like the primary path, I expect MSI-X failure to change vector policy, not queue identity so that device-advertised admin queues do not silently move under INTx fallback.
records.implementation_note: MSI-X preserves `avq->vq_index`; INTx currently increments the running queue counter instead.
conditions_of_satisfaction: Both MSI-X and INTx paths query the advertised admin queue index before creating the queue.
The admin queue creation call uses the same transport-advertised index in every routing mode.
Only driver-requested queues are permitted to remain purely sequential across fallback modes.
alternative_paths: Shared and shared-slow MSI-X policies may share vectors, but they must still preserve queue numbering.

records.title: Slow-path queues must remain bound to their original interrupt-dispatch list across queue reset and re-enable
records.summary: Queue reset and re-enable must preserve slow-path queue topology instead of defaulting every callback queue into the regular list.
records.description: Initial list placement in `drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_common.c:225-230` and `:338-356` distinguishes slow-path queues, while post-reset re-enable in `drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_modern.c:653-656` always targets `virtqueues`.
records.user_story: As a transport maintainer recovering a queue after reset, I expect callback routing to stay on the same topology so that a reset does not silently convert a slow-path queue into a regular-queue interrupt source.
records.implementation_note: Initial placement is vector-aware; current post-reset reattachment is not.
conditions_of_satisfaction: Initial queue setup records or derives whether a queue belongs to the slow-path list.
Queue reset removes the queue from the correct current list before recovery.
Re-enable restores the queue to the same dispatch list implied by its original vector policy.
alternative_paths: Per-vq MSI-X queues with dedicated vectors may bypass the slow-path list, but callback-bearing slow-path queues cannot.

records.title: Transport feature finalization must preserve every transport feature the transport later claims to support, or clear it as unsupported
records.summary: Shared feature filtering and transport-specific post-processing must not disagree silently about feature support.
records.user_story: As a maintainer reasoning about transport feature support, I expect every feature bit that a transport later advertises to survive finalization intentionally so that one file cannot claim support that another file silently removed.
records.implementation_note: The shared whitelist is the default filter; transports may restore or explicitly clear bits afterward.
conditions_of_satisfaction: The shared whitelist is treated as a closed set backed by `quality/mechanical/vring_transport_features_cases.txt`.
Any transport that relies on `VIRTIO_F_RING_RESET` or `VIRTIO_F_ADMIN_VQ` restores or preserves those bits explicitly.
Requirements and review artifacts distinguish features defined in headers from features actually preserved by finalization code.
alternative_paths: A transport may explicitly clear a public bit as unsupported, but it must do so intentionally rather than by unnoticed fallthrough.

records.title: Queue-reset helper state must use the correct metadata owner for normal and admin queues
records.summary: Queue-reset helpers must select queue metadata ownership the same way teardown does for normal and admin queues.
records.description: Teardown special-cases admin queues in `drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_common.c:27-35` and `:271-285`, while reset helpers in `drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_modern.c:600-603` and `:640-641` always index `vp_dev->vqs[]`.
records.user_story: As a maintainer enabling queue-local reset, I expect admin queues to use their dedicated metadata slot so that reset helpers do not index the wrong array entry or dereference absent queue metadata.
records.implementation_note: Admin-queue ownership already exists in teardown and should be mirrored by reset helpers.
conditions_of_satisfaction: Queue-reset helpers special-case admin queues the same way teardown already does.
Array-based lookup is used only for ordinary queue indices represented in `vp_dev->vqs[]`.
Reset-time list removal and reattachment use the metadata owner that actually stores the queue node and vector state.
alternative_paths: Ordinary queues may continue to use `vp_dev->vqs[index]`; admin queues must use `vp_dev->admin_vq.info`.

records.title: Wakeup-enabled MMIO IRQ setup and teardown must be symmetric
records.summary: MMIO wake-specific IRQ setup must be mirrored by wake-specific teardown before the IRQ is freed.
records.description: The symmetry gap is between `drivers/virtio/virtio_mmio.c:446-465` and `drivers/virtio/virtio_mmio.c:328-337`.
records.user_story: As a platform integrator using wake-capable MMIO devices, I expect wake configuration to unwind cleanly on teardown so that reprobe and power-state transitions do not inherit stale wake-enabled IRQ state.
records.implementation_note: The setup path conditionally calls `enable_irq_wake(irq)` but teardown currently frees the IRQ directly.
conditions_of_satisfaction: Wake-specific IRQ setup is guarded by the same condition the transport uses to enable wake.
Teardown disables IRQ wake before `free_irq()` when wake had been enabled.
The same symmetry holds on error cleanup after partial queue creation.
alternative_paths: Platforms without `wakeup-source` may skip wake-specific handling, but teardown must still be correct when wake was enabled.

records.title: Runtime error-state signaling via VIRTIO_CONFIG_S_NEEDS_RESET must remain visible through the ordinary configuration-notification path

citation.citation_excerpt:
- Device SHOULD set bit 64 when entering unrecoverable error state
- If DRIVER_OK is set, device MUST send configuration change notification
- Driver SHOULD NOT rely on completion of in-flight operations

records.summary: An unrecoverable device state must stay observable through the public status bit and the documented config-notification path.
records.description: The public status bit is defined in `include/uapi/linux/virtio_config.h:42-44`; explored runtime handlers forward config changes through `virtio_config_changed()` without masking that signal.
records.user_story: As a driver author servicing runtime notifications, I expect an unrecoverable device state to stay observable through the documented status bit and config callback so that I can make a driver-specific recovery decision.
records.implementation_note: The explored transport paths already deliver config notifications; the shared transport/core contract is to preserve that signal rather than to implement driver policy itself.
conditions_of_satisfaction: `VIRTIO_CONFIG_S_NEEDS_RESET` remains a driver-visible public status bit (`include/uapi/linux/virtio_config.h:42-44`).

If `DRIVER_OK` is set, the shared transport/core path preserves the configuration-change notification channel that lets drivers re-read status (`drivers/virtio/virtio.c:126-145`, `drivers/virtio/virtio_mmio.c:296-303`, `drivers/virtio/virtio_pci_common.c:73-79`).
Shared transport/core code does not silently clear or remap the condition before driver-visible config handling runs.
alternative_paths: Specific drivers may react differently after reading the bit, but MMIO and PCI must both preserve the ordinary config-notification path that exposes it.

records.title: Queue setup must finish transport address programming and enable the queue before the driver adds buffers

citation.citation_excerpt:
virtio_device_ready() is used to enable the virtqueue and to
notify the device that the driver is ready to manage the device
("DRIVER_OK").

In any case, the virtqueues need to be enabled before adding buffers to
them.

records.summary: Queue lifecycle code must not allow drivers to submit work until transport address programming is complete and the queue has been enabled.
records.description: The cited setup contract is visible in `drivers/virtio/virtio_mmio.c:390-429` and the driver documentation at `formal_docs/writing_virtio_drivers.txt:121-130`.
records.user_story: As a virtio driver author adding queues, I expect setup to enforce the documented order so that buffers are never submitted to an unready or unaddressed queue.
records.implementation_note: MMIO explicitly writes queue addresses before `QUEUE_READY`, and the driver documentation requires virtqueues to be enabled before adding buffers.
conditions_of_satisfaction: Queue setup writes queue size and descriptor addresses before exposing the queue as ready (`drivers/virtio/virtio_mmio.c:390-429`).
Drivers do not add buffers until the queue has been enabled and the device is ready (`formal_docs/writing_virtio_drivers.txt:121-130`).
Legacy MMIO activation paths still complete transport address programming before queue use begins.
alternative_paths: Legacy MMIO uses PFN-based activation instead of `QUEUE_READY`, but still must complete address programming before queue use.

records.title: vDPA queue discovery must apply affinity to the same transport queue index it instantiated
records.summary: When vDPA queue discovery skips unused virtqueue_info slots, affinity programming must follow the compact queue index that was actually instantiated.
records.description: The queue-discovery path in `drivers/virtio/virtio_vdpa.c:347-363` compacts queue creation through `queue_idx++`, and the steady-state affinity helper at `drivers/virtio/virtio_vdpa.c:407-418` shows that `vq->index` is the authoritative transport-facing identity.
records.user_story: As a transport maintainer wiring sparse virtqueue layouts into a vDPA backend, I expect queue affinity to follow the queue that was actually created so that skipped descriptor slots do not silently pin the wrong queue to the wrong CPU mask.
records.implementation_note: The implementation must keep setup-time affinity programming aligned with the same queue index namespace later exposed by `virtqueue.index`.
conditions_of_satisfaction: Unused `virtqueue_info[]` entries may remain sparse (`include/linux/virtio_config.h:22-33`).
Queue creation translates sparse descriptor slots into compact queue indices before setup-time affinity programming runs (`drivers/virtio/virtio_vdpa.c:350-356`).
The affinity callback uses the same queue index namespace that the resulting `virtqueue` exposes via `vq->index` (`drivers/virtio/virtio_vdpa.c:407-418`).
alternative_paths: Drivers that provide no affinity descriptor or run on backends without `set_vq_affinity` may skip affinity programming, but sparse queue numbering must still be correct when the capability exists.

records.title: Any probe failure after virtio_device_ready() must reset the device before deleting virtqueues

citation.citation_excerpt:
virtio_device_ready() is used to enable the virtqueue and to
notify the device that the driver is ready to manage the device
("DRIVER_OK").

In any case, the virtqueues need to be enabled before adding buffers to
them.

records.summary: Once probe has crossed into live DRIVER_OK state, any later failure path must reset the device before deleting virtqueues or freeing queue-owned state.
records.description: The late failure path in `drivers/virtio/virtio_input.c:321-339` comes after `virtio_device_ready()`, while the ordinary remove and freeze paths at `drivers/virtio/virtio_input.c:349-376` both reset the device before deleting queues.
records.user_story: As a virtio driver author whose probe hits a late registration failure, I expect teardown to quiesce the live device before queue memory is released so that the transport cannot still treat freed queues as active.
records.implementation_note: The probe-error path must mirror the reset-before-del_vqs ordering already used by ordinary remove/freeze teardown once the device has reached DRIVER_OK.
conditions_of_satisfaction: Probe code identifies whether the device has already crossed into live `DRIVER_OK` state before late failure unwinds run (`drivers/virtio/virtio_input.c:321-325`).

Any post-ready failure path calls `virtio_reset_device()` before deleting virtqueues or freeing queue-owned buffers (`drivers/virtio/virtio_input.c:330-339`, contrasted with `drivers/virtio/virtio_input.c:349-376`).
The post-ready error path mirrors the same reset-before-del_vqs ordering used by ordinary remove/freeze teardown.
alternative_paths: Failures that happen before `virtio_device_ready()` may delete setup state directly, but once the live transition happens the reset barrier is mandatory.
